title: Arrays
description: Store lists of values and use core array methods like push, map, and filter.
objectives: Index, push, and iterate over array elements
recap: Arrays hold an ordered list of values — you access them by index (`arr[0]`), check their size with `.length`, and iterate with `for` or `for...of`. Methods like `.map`, `.filter`, and `.reduce` transform arrays without manual looping.
practicePrompts: Given an array of numbers, return only the positive ones.
Count how many words in an array start with the letter `a`.

practiceExercises.title: Positives only
practiceExercises.prompt: Write a function `positivesOnly(arr)` that takes an array of numbers and returns a new array containing only the positive numbers (> 0).
practiceExercises.goal: Use .filter to keep only elements matching a condition.
hints: You want to keep only some elements, not transform them.
Use `.filter(callback)` — the callback returns `true` to keep an element.

practiceExercises.title: Count words starting with a
practiceExercises.prompt: Write a function `countStartsWithA(words)` that takes an array of strings and returns how many of them start with the lowercase letter `a`.
hints: `word[0]` is the first character of a string — just like an array.
Filter the array to keep only words whose first character is 'a', then return `.length`.

practiceExercises.title: Maximum in an array
practiceExercises.prompt: Write a function `maxInArray(nums)` that returns the largest number in a non-empty array. You may NOT use `Math.max`.
practiceExercises.goal: Track a running max by iterating through the array.
hints: Start with `let best = nums[0];` — the first element is a safe starting guess.
Loop over the rest; whenever you see something bigger, update `best`.
`for (const n of nums) { if (n > best) best = n; } return best;`
